Facts.text: "The Great Invasion" sees a vast horde of Vandals, Alans, and Suebians cross the Rhine at Mainz, beginning the invasion of Gallia
Byzantine General Belisarius completes the conquest of Sicily, defeating Ostrogothic garrison of Syracuse, and ending his consulship for the year
Coffin of Ho-tse Shen-hui interred in a stupa built in China
Skirmish at Englefield: Ethelred of Wessex beats a section of the Viking Great Heathen Army, camped at Reading
James I of Aragon the Conqueror enters Medina Mayurqa (Palma) consummating Christian conquest of the island of Majorca
100,000 Jews expelled from Sicily
Cesare Borgia (son of pope Alexander VI) occupies Urbino
Dutch East India Company Admiral Steven van der Haghen's fleet reaches Bantam, Indonesia
France & Brandenburg sign secret treaty
France & England sign Boyne-treaty
De Italiaanse Opera (The Italian Opera) theater opens on the Leidsegracht in Amsterdam with "La Fatiche d'Ercole per Deianira" by PA Ziani
1st Huguenots depart France to Cape of Good Hope
A window tax is imposed in England, causing many shopkeepers to brick up their windows to avoid the tax.
Genroku earthquake off the coast of Japan, near Edo (modern-day Tokyo), kills thousands and triggers a large tsunami, killing thousands more
Russia joins the Alliance of Versailles
British expeditionary army occupies Goree (Dakar) Senegal
Rhode Island establishes wage & price controls to curb inflation: Limit is 70 cents a day for carpenters, 42 cents for tailors
British fleet beat Dutch Merchant vessels
Bank of North America, 1st US bank opens
The Dutch East Indies Company is formally dissolved, taken over by the Batavian Republic
End of French Republican calendar; France returns to Gregorianism
Alabama becomes 1st state to license dental surgeons
Dutch colony in Dutch Indies counts 4,800 slaves
A world record 22,990 mm of rain falls in a single year in Cherrapunji, Assam, India
-Jan 20th) Battle of Stone's River (Battle of Murfreesboro), Tennessee in US Civil War
Skirmish at Parker Cross Roads, Tennessee
Union ironclad ship "Monitor" sank off Cape Hatteras, North Carolina
J D Schneiter patents rocket mail in France, (not done)
Cornerstone laid for Honolulu's Iolani Palace (only royal palace in US)
Edison gives 1st public demonstration of his incandescent lamp
Brooklyn's last day as a city, it incorporates into NYC (1/1/1898)
In the first election under their new constitution, Cuba elects a Congress and their first president, Tomas Estrada Palma
First New Year's Eve celebration held in Times Square, New York City
French, British and Italian treaty concerning rights on Abyssinia
For 1st time, a ball drops at Times Square to signal the New Year
US tobacco industry produced 9 billion cigarettes in 1910
Dutch Social-democratic trade union NVV counts 159,450 members
Victorian all-rounder Roy Park makes Test cricket debut for Australia v England in 2nd Test in Melbourne; bowled by Harry Howell for a first ball 'golden duck' in his first and only Test innings
Last San Francisco firehorses retired
1st transatlantic radio broadcast of a voice, Pittsburgh-Manchester
BBC begins using Big Ben chime ID
Harry Tierney and Joseph McCarthy's musical "Kid Boots," premieres in New York City
14th congress of CPSU decides to accelerate industry
Ponsford scores 336 against SA, giving him 1146 for month
Pontifical encyclical Casti connubii against mixed marriages
US tobacco industry produced 123 billion cigarettes in 1930
City of Paris accepts donation of the writings gathered by feminist publisher Marguerite Durand and establishes a library (Bibliothèque Marguerite Durand) for the collection
John P O'Brien sworn-in as mayor of NYC
Helen Richey becomes 1st woman to pilot an airmail transport
Charles Darrow patents the board game Monopoly, goes on to be the 1st millionaire game designer
CPH becomes Dutch Communist Party
Daniel Sternefeld conducts premiere of his ballet "Pierlala" at the Royal Flemish Opera of Antwerp; choreographer Wladimir Karnetzky also danced the title role
Dr R N Harger's "drunkometer", 1st breath test, introduced in Indiana
Dutch national debt hits ƒ3,986,629,805.70
Dutch national debt hits ƒ4,218,553,180.99
Young Park (2) in the Bronx named in honor of Samuel Young
Battle of the Barents Sea between British Navy and German Kriegsmarine off North Cape, Norway
48 people die in a train accident in Ogden, Utah
Japanese army evacuates harbor city Akyab
World War II: Hungary declares war on Germany.
French troops leave Lebanon
Dutch police actions up Java gone on strike
18 countries recognize Republic Indonesia
1st battery to convert radioactive energy to electricity announced
American jockeyBill Shoemakershatters record, riding 485 winners in a year
Hulan Jack sworn in as Manhattan Borough president
WFBC (now WYFF) TV channel 4 in G'ville-Spartanburg, SC (NBC) begins
Bill Shoemaker1st jockey to win national riding championship 4 times
International Geophysical Year scientific project ends after 18 months
Failed coup by Syrian group in Lebanon
Marshall Plan expires after distributing more than $12 billion
The Beach Boys play their debut gig under that name
"Match Game" debuts on NBC with host Gene Rayburn
American Basketball League announces suspension of operation
Dutch leave New Guinea
Katanga becomes part of Democratic Republic of Congo
Ohio ends suit against Reds when they agree to stay in Cin for 10 yrs
Dear Abby show premieres on CBS radio (runs 11 years)
Donald Campbell (UK) sets world water speed record (276.33 mph)
Indonesia proclaims expelled from the UN
Pirate Radio 390 (Radio Invicata) off England, resumes transmitting
Test Cricket debut of Bishen Singh Bedi, India v WI Calcutta, 2-92
Toboggan Chutes begin operation in Cleveland Metroparks
1st NBA game at Great Western Forum, LA Lakers beat Houston 147-118
New York Jets win AFL championship
Record high barometric pressure (1083.8 mb, 32 inches) recorded at Agata in Siberia, Soviet Union; eclipsed in December 2001
Soviet TU-144 makes its maiden flight, the first by a commercial supersonic airliner
Congo-Brazzaville becomes People's republic, under major Ngouabi
President Allende nationalizes Chilean coal mines
Would have been start of Aust/Eng Test Cricket at MCG, washed out
Edmund Compton, then Northern Ireland Ombudsman, is replaced by John Benn
KAID TV channel 4 in Boise, ID (PBS) begins broadcasting
Lt Gen Robert E Cushman, Jr, USMC, ends term as deputy director of CIA
DDT, first of the modern insecticides used against malaria and typhus, is banned by the US Environmental Protection Agency after evidence of adverse environmental side effects
Leap second day; also in 1973-79, 1987

Ballon d'Or: Ajax forwardJohan Cruyffwins back-to-back awards, and his 3rd overall, for best European football player ahead of Bayern Munich defender Franz Beckenbauer and Legia Warsaw midfielder Kazimierz Deyna
Gold legal in US, Franklin Mint strikes Panama's Gold 100 balboa coin
New York Yankees sign Cy Young Award winner Jim "Catfish" Hunter for 5 years for a record $3.75 million from the Oakland A's
Popular Electronics displays Altair 8800 computer
The Cars played their 1st gig
TV soap "Somerset" ends 6 year run
Amir Sheikh Jabir al-Ahmad al-Jabir Al Sabah becomes leader of Kuwait
Cambodia drops diplomatic relations with Vietnam
Donald Woods, a banned anti-Apartheid white editor flees South Africa
Kenyan writer Ngũgĩ wa Thiong'o is imprisoned for co-writing a play critical of the Kenyan government
WFAT (Brooklyn New York pirate radio station) begins broadcasting on 1620 AM
CIA director, Admiral Stansfield Turner retires from the Navy
Iran shah names Shapour Bakhtiar as Prime Minister
Taiwan's final day of diplomatic relations with US
The Runaways' band's last ever concert at the Cow Palace in Daly City, California

Winterland Ballroom in San Francisco, California closes after 556 concerts; Final show is the Grateful Dead, New Riders of the Purple Sage, and The Blues Brothers; program lasts for over 8 hours, with the Grateful Dead playing for nearly 6 hours; concert broadcast on local PBS TV station KQED, and simulcast by radio station KSAN-FM, after the show, the crowd was treated to a hot, buffet-style breakfast.
A Jewish owned hotel in Nairobi Kenya is bombed killing 18
After being struck by an orange thrown from the crowd West Indian fast bowler Sylvester Clarke knocks out a spectator with a brick during 4th Test against Pakistan in Multan
NY Islanders greatest shutout margin (9-0) vs Chicago Black Hawks
Senegal president Leopold Senghor resigns
CNN Headline News cable TV channel debuts in US
Lt Jerry Rawlings becomes head of Ghana, suspends constitution
Netherlands unemployment stands at record 475,000
CBS Mystery Theater final episode on radio after 8 years
NBC radio cancels almost all of its network daily features
TV soap "Doctors" ends 19 year run
Brunei gains complete independence from Britain
Jose Happart installed as mayor of Voeren, Belgium
Nigeria's National Assembly dissolves after military coup
Def Leppard drummer Rick Allen loses his arm in a car crash
NYC subway gunman Bernhard Goetz surrenders to police in NH
Test Cricket debut of Mohammad Azharuddin, v England at Calcutta
US leaves UNESCO
Dupont Plaza Hotel fire in San Juan, Puerto Rico kills 97
WIS-AM in Columbia, South Carolina changes call letters to WVOC (now WOMG)
Iraq begins a military draft of 17-year-olds
Sci-Fi Channel on cable TV begins transmitting
United Somali Congress seizes Presidential Palace
CPN, Communist Party of Netherland - last day of existence
Daniel R. McCarthy elected NY Yankee managing general partner
Dow Jones Industrial Average closes at record high 3168.83
J. Donald Crump resigns as CFL Commissioner
Anti Apartheid Group of Netherlands (AABN) disbands
Date skipped in Kiribati as the Phoenix Islands and Line Islands change time zones from UTC-11 to UTC+13 and UTC-10 to UTC+14, respectively
First snowless December in Baltimore, Maryland
Cartoonist Bill Watterson ends his "Calvin & Hobbes" comic strip after 10 years, believing he had achieved all he could in the medium
Matthew Elliott scores separate cricket century same day for Victoria
72 year-old Marv Levy retires as coach of the NFL Buffalo Bills after 12 seasons, including 4 consecutive losing Super Bowl appearances

Buffalo's Czech NHL goalie Dominik Hasek stops 36 shots for his 27th career shutout in Sabres' 3-0 win over Ottawa Senators; his 6th shutout in month of December; first since George Hainsworth in 1928-29 to achieve the feat
Microsoft buys Hotmail email service for $400 million and re-launches it as MSN Hotmail
More Swedes died than were born in 1997, 1st time since 1809
Orlando Hernandez, half-brother of pitcher Livan, defects from Cuba
South African & US surgeons separate Zambian Siamese twins joined at the head
Exchange rates between the euro and legacy currencies in the Eurozone become fixed.
US movie box office hits record $6.24 billion for year
Control of Panama Canal reverts to Panama
Official opening of Taipei 101, then the tallest skyscraper in the world, at a height of 509 metres (1,670 feet)
The final contest at the old Lansdowne Road Stadium in Dublin is a rugby match with Leinster beating Ulster 20-12 in a Celtic League game
Bocaue Fire. Seven people injured when a fire razed several fireworks stores in the Municipality of Bocaue, Bulacan, Philippines.
The Massive Big Dig construction project in Boston, Massachusetts ends.
36 die and 47 are wounded during a stampede of New Year revellers on Shanghai's Bund riverfront
82nd Orange Bowl: #1 Clemson beats #4 Oklahoma, 37-17
US law enforcement kills 1,134 in 2015, young black men 9 times more likely to be victims
Ban on ivory trade in China comes into effect
Bus crash at Migaa near Molo, central Kenya, kills at least 30
Cleveland Browns crash to a 28-24 loss to Pittsburgh Steelers at Heinz Field to become only the 2nd team in NFL history to finish season with 0-16 record

Houston guard James Harden scores 43 points in Rockets' 113-101 win over Memphis Grizzlies; 4th straight NBA game with 40+ points and 8th straight with 35+; joins Oscar Robertson as only player with at least 35 points & 5 assists in 8 straight games
Iraqi militiamen and protesters breach the outer wall of the US embassy in Baghdad following US airstrikes against Kataib Hezbollah, an Iranian-backed Iraqi militia, on December 29
WHO grants the Pfizer/BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine emergency authorization, paving the way for worldwide distribution
Trinidad and Tobago declare a state of emergency over gang violence, which has resulted in one of the highest homicide rates in Latin America
Roman EmperorCommodussurvives poisoning attempt by his mistress only to be strangled in the bath in assassination plot
William I of Orangedemands freedom of religious conscience for his subjects in dramatic speech to the Council of State
British East India Company receives its royal charter fromElizabeth Ito explore trade routes in the East Indies
Hungarian King Bethlen Gabor and Holy Roman EmperorFerdinand IIsign the Treaty of Mikulov, ending war between them
James IIof England is named Duke of Normandy by Louis XIV of France.
John Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough, fired as English army commander
English astronomerJames Bradleyannounces the discovery of Earth's nutation, or wobble
Queen Victoriachooses Ottawa as new capital of Canada
PresidentAbraham Lincolnsigns act admitting West Virginia to the Union
Gustav Mahlerconducts the Metropolitan Opera, in New York City
Marie Curiereceives her second Nobel Prize, this time in Chemistry, for her work with radioactivity
Italian FascistMussoliniorders the suppression of opposition newspapers
PopePius XIpublishes encyclical Divini illius magistri
US PresidentHarry Trumanofficially proclaims end of WW II
American thoroughbred jockeysBill Shoemakerand Joe Culmone end the year tied, leading the nation with record 388 wins

Danny Nardico stops former world middleweight championJake LaMottain 7 rounds in a light heavyweight non-title bout in Coral Gables, Florida; LaMotta is knocked down for the only time in his career, his corner stopping fight after the round
TheGeneral Motors Corporationbecomes the first U.S. corporation to make over $1 billion USD in a year.
AAU awardsBobby Morrow, James Sullivan Memorial Trophy
Cuban dictatorFulgencio Batistainforms his cabinet he is fleeing the country
Jerry Garciaand Bob Weir of The Grateful Dead play music together for the 1st time at Dana Morgan's Music Store in Palo Alto, California
The Monkees single "I'm a Believer", written byNeil Diamond, hits #1 and stays there for 7 weeks
AFL Championship, Alameda County Coliseum, Oakland: Oakland Raiders beat Houston Oilers 40-7;George Blanda4 field goals for Raiders in club's 1st AFL Championship
Evel Knievelfails in his attempt to jump the Caesar's Palace Fountain, Las Vegas, breaking his pelvis, femur, wrist, hip and both ankles
Paul McCartneyfiles a lawsuit to dissolve The Beatles in a dispute over Allen Klein's management of the group
US Congress authorizesEisenhowerdollar coin
Martin McGuinnessis arrested and held under the new Republic of Ireland legislation
Johan Cruyffchosen European Football Player of theYear
Singer-songwriters Lindsey Buckingham andStevie Nicksjoin rock bandFleetwood Mac
Ted Bundyescapes from jail in Colorado
Bruce Springsteenand the E-Street Band's "The River Tour" concert at Nassau Coliseum (Uniondale, New York) clocks in at almost 4 hours
Benjamin Ward appointed 1st African American NY City Police Commissioner by MayorEd Koch
President Shagari of Nigeria is overthrown in a military coup and Major GeneralMuhammadu Buhariis installed as Head of State

Pittsburgh NHL centerMario Lemieuxaccounts for all his team's goals in Penguins 8-6 win over the NJ Devils; famously scores 5 goals, each in a different way: even strength, power play, shorthanded, penalty shot and empty net; also 3 assists
JockeyKent Desormeauxsets a record with 598 wins in a single year
Barbra Streisandperforms her 1st public concert in 20 years before a sell-out crows of over 25,000 at MGM Grand Garden Arena in Las Vegas, Nevada
Largest free concert in the world occurs whenRod Stewartperforms in front of four million people at Copacabana Beach, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil
Intelcuts price of Pentium II-233 MHz from $401 to $268
Boris Yeltsinresigns as President of Russia, leaving Prime MinisterVladimir Putinas acting President
Jamie Dimonnamed as CEO of JPMorgan Chase
Beji Caid Essebsiis sworn in as Tunisia's 1st ever freely elected President
Chris Ofiliis appointed Commander of the Order of the British Empire (CBE) in the 2017 New Year Honours for services to art
Indian movie starRajinikanthannounces he is entering politics
SingerLordecalled a bigot in ad in Washington Post after cancelling show in Israel
Prime MinisterSheikh Hasinawins third term in Bangladesh elections with 17 people killed in violent clashes
QueenMargrethe IIof Denmark (83) announces her plan to abdicate the throne on her 52nd anniversary of ascension on January 14
Frisia and Groningen adopt Gregorian calendar, tomorrow is 1/12/1701

Battle of Quebec: American Continental Army led by Richard Montgomery is defeated trying to take the British stronghold of Quebec City in the American Revolutionary War, General Montgomery is killed andBenedict Arnoldis injured
Ratification of United Nations Charter completed

A heavy, dense fog rolls over Soldier Field in Chicago during the second quarter of the Bears vs. Eagles NFC Divisional Playoff Game, cutting visibility to 15-20 yards in a game known as "The Fog Bowl" (Bears win 20-12)
Muhammad bin Qasim, Umayyad military commander who defeated Persia, and 1st Governor of al-Sind (712-15), born in Ta'if, Umayyad Caliphate (d. 715)
Pope Callixtus III [Alfonso de Borgia], head of the Catholic Church & ruler of the Papal States (1455-58), born in Kingdom of Valencia (d. 1458)
Silvio Antoniano, Italian cardinal and theologist (Tre libri), born in Rome (d. 1603)
Henri I de Lorraine, Duke of Guise and French leader of the Catholic League during the French Wars of Religion, born in Joinville, Kingdom of France (d. 1588)[1]
Simon Forman, English Elizabethan astrologer and occultist, born in Quidhampton, England (d. 1611)[1]
Go-Yozei, 107th Emperor of Japan (1586-1611) and last of the Edo period, born in Japan (d. 1617)
Giulio Cesare Monteverdi, Italian organist and composer (Deh chi tace il bel pensero), baptized in Cremona, Italy (d. 1630 or 31)
Hermannus Boerhaave, Dutch botanist and physician, born in Voorhout, Netherlands (d. 1738)
Yoriyuki Arima, Japanese mathematician (approximated the value of pi and its square, pi^2), born in Kurume, Kyūshū, Japan (d. 1783)
Franz Joseph Oehlschlagel, Czech organist and composer, born in Lahošť, Czech Republic (d. 1788)
Isabella Maria of Parma, wife of future Holy Roman emperor, born in Madrid, Spain (d. 1763)

Thomas Taglichsbeck, German violinist and composer, born in Ansbach, Germany (d. 1867)

Henry Hiles, English composer, born in Shrewsbury (d. 1904)
Isma'il Pasha, Khedive of Egypt (1863-79), born in Cairo, Egypt (d. 1895)
Emile Loubet, French politician (President 1899-1906), born in Marsanne, France (d. 1929)
Ferdinand Domela Nieuwenhuis, Dutch socialist politician (1st Socialist Member of Parliament), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1919)
Richard Kleinmichel, German pianist, composer (Manon), and arranger (Wagner operas for piano), born in Posen, Prussian Grand Duchy of Posen (now Poznań, Poland) (d. 1901)
Giovanni Pascoli, Italian classicist & poet, born in San Mauro Pascoli, Italy (d. 1912)

Michael "King" Kelly, American Baseball HOF utility (NL batting champion 1884, 86; NL runs scored 1884–86, Chicago White Sox) and manager (Boston Beaneaters, Reds; Cincinnati KKs), born in Troy, NY (d. 1894)
Max Fiedler, German conductor and composer, born in Zittau, Saxony, (d. 1939)
Joseph S. Cullinan, American oil industrialist who helped build the Texas oil industry (The Texas Company), born in Pennsylvania, United States (d. 1937)
Alfredo Panzini, Italian author (Dizionario Moderno), born in Senigallia, Italy (d. 1939)

Ernest Austin, English composer, born in Poplar, London (d. 1947)

Elizabeth Arden, Canadian businesswoman, born in Woodbridge, Ontario (d. 1966)
Horacio Quiroga, Uruguayan playwright, poet, and short story writer (Anaconda, El Crimen del Otro), born in Salto, Uruguay (d. 1937)
Colin G. Fink, American chemist (electro chemistry), born in Hoboken, New Jersey (d. 1953)[1]
Jacob Israël de Haan, Dutch poet/writer (Pipelines, Jewish Song), born in Holland, Netherlands (d. 1924)
Max Pechstein, German "entartet" painter and graphic artist (The Bridge), born in Zwickau, Germany (d. 1955)
Ben A. Jones, American horse trainer (US Champion trainer by earnings 1941, 43, 44, 52; Kentucky Derby x 6 including Citation, Whirlaway), born in Parnell, Missouri (d. 1961)
David Cohen, Dutch historian and chairman of the Jewish Council, born in Deventer, Holland (d. 1967)
Princess Victoria Adelaide of Schleswig-Holstein, born in Province of Schleswig-Holstein (d. 1970)
Jason Robards Sr, American actor (Acapulco, Isle of the Dead), born in Hillsdale, Michigan (d. 1963)
Jimmy Blanckenberg, South African cricket medium pace bowler (18 Tests, 60 wickets; Western Province), born in Cape Town, South Africa (d. 1955)

Ernest John Moeran, English composer, born in London Borough of Hounslow, United Kingdom (d. 1950)

Lionel Daunais, Quebec singer and composer, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1982)
Chuck Gardiner, Canadian NHL goalie (Vezina winner), born in Edinburgh, United Kingdom (d. 1934)
Tadeusz Breza, Polish writer (The Bronze Gate), born in Sekežintsõ, Ostrog (d. 1970)
Erna Bogen-Bogáti, Hungarian foils (Olympic bronze 1932), born in Jaroslaw, Poland (d. 2002)

Marko Rothmuller, Croatian composer, born in Slavonski Brod, Croatia (d. 1993)

Roy Rowland, American film director, born in New York City (d. 1995)

Pat Brady [Robert Ellsworth O'Brady], American actor (The Roy Rogers Show, Man from Music Mountain), born in Toledo, Ohio (d. 1972)
Suzy Delair, French actress (The Murderer Lives at Number 21; Utopia; White Paws), and singer, born in Paris, France (d. 2020)
Joan McCracken, American actress, dancer and comedian (Claudie Story of a Marriage), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1961)
Artur Fischer, German inventor with more patents than Edison, born in Tumlingen, Baden-Württemberg (d. 2016)
Recy Taylor, American civil rights activist and sexual assault victim, born in Abbeville, Alabama (d. 2017)
Tommy Byrne, American baseball pitcher (MLB All-Star 1950; World Series 1949, 56; NY Yankees), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 2007)

Vicki Draves, American diver (Olympic gold platform & springboard 1948), born in San Francisco, California (d. 2010)

Dieter Noll, German writer (Die Abenteuer des Werner Holt), born in Riesa, Saxony (d. 2008)
Amarillo Slim [Thomas Preston], American hall of fame professional poker player (1971 World Series main event), born in Amarillo, Texas (d. 2012)
Hugh McElhenny, American Pro/College Football HOF halfback (University of Washington; 5 × First-team All-Pro; 6 × Pro Bowl; Minnesota Vikings, SF 49ers), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2022)

Siné [Maurice Sinet], French cartoonist, born in Paris, France (d. 2016)
Veijo Meri, Finnish writer (Private Jokinen’s Marriage Leave), born in Viipuri, Finland (now Vyborg, Russia) (d. 2015)
George Schlatter, American television producer and director (as Rowan & Martin's Laugh-In; Real People; American Comedy Awards), born in Birmingham, Alabama
Mies Bouman, Dutch television presenter (Open the Village), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 2018)
Peter May, English cricket batsman (66 Tests, 4,537 @ 46.77, 13 x 100, 22 x 50; HS 285no; Surrey CCC), born in Reading, England (d. 1994)
Sidney Greenbaum, British scholar, born in London (d. 1996)
Odetta [Holmes], American folk singer (Sanctuary), actress and civil rights activist, born in Birmingham, Alabama (d. 2008)
Bob Shaw, Northern Irish sci-fi author (Light of Other Days, The Ragged Astronauts), born in Belfast, Northern Ireland (d. 1996)
Gil Mellé, American jazz saxophonist and composer, born in New York City (d. 2004)
Xavier Chamorro Cardenal, Nicaraguan journalist and newspaper editor (La Prensa; El Nuevo Diario), born in Granada, Nicaragua, (d. 2008)
Chryssa [Vardea-Mavromichali], Greek-American artist, born in Athens, Greece (d. 2013)
Edward Bunker, American Crime novelist (No Beast So Fierce; Animal Factory), actor (Straight Time; Reservoir Dogs), screenwriter, and felon, born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2005)
Fred F. Carter Jr., American country session guitarist, singer, producer and composer (Nashville's "A-Team"), born in Winnsboro, Louisiana (d. 2010)
Akram Awan, Islamic scholar & spiritual leader, born in Chakwal, Pakistan (d. 2017)
Bette Nash, American airline flight attendant for record 66 years (1957-2023), born in Pleasantville, New Jersey (d. 2024)[1]
Peter Allan, Australian cricket fast bowler (1 Test, 2 wickets; Queensland), born in Brisbane, Australia (d. 2023)
Rolf Haufs, German writer, born in Düsseldorf (d. 2013)
Ron Northcott, Canadian curler (World C'ship gold 1966, 68, 69), born in Innisfail, Alberta (d. 2023)
Jerry Sturm, American football tackle (AFL All-Star 1964, 66; Denver Broncos, NO Saints), born in English, Indiana (d. 2020)
Avram Hershko, Hungarian-born Israeli biochemist and Nobel laureate in Chemistry, born in Karcag, Hungary

Oleg Anatolyevich Yakovlev, Russian cosmonaut, born in Leningrad
Tim Considine, American actor (Spin & Marty; My Three Sons, 1960-65 - "Mike"), and photographer, born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2022)
Sarah Miles, English actress (Ryan's Daughter; Hope and Glory; The Man Who Loved Cat Dancing), born in Ingatestone, Essex, England
Andy Summers, English rock guitarist (Police - "Roxanne"), born in Blackpool, Lancashire
Nick Hedges, British photographer known for documenting the lives of poor people, born in Bromsgrove, England (d. 2025)
Pete Quaife, British rock bassist (The Kinks, 1963-69 - "Sunny Afternoon"), and cartoonist, born in Tavistock, Devonshire, England (d. 2010)
